title: Carestream DRX-Revolution Mobile X-Ray System - Wireless Detector Not Connecting or Not Detected
description: Wireless detector connection failures, detector not detected messages, failed pairing, battery issues, or workstation communication problems.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the mobile X-ray system from patient use if the detector is not detected and imaging cannot be completed reliably. Action: Use another mobile X-ray unit or detector if imaging is clinically urgent. Do not continue repeated exposure attempts when the detector is not connected. Expected outcome: Patient care continues without relying on an unreliable imaging setup. Why it matters: A detector communication failure can delay care, create incomplete exams, or increase the chance of repeat imaging.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Problem
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the issue is detector detection, wireless connection, image transfer, or workstation recognition. Action: Check the system display for messages such as detector not found, detector disconnected, detector unavailable, or acquisition failure. Ask when the issue occurs: At startup. After selecting an exam. When preparing exposure. After exposure. Only with one detector. Only in certain rooms or areas. Expected outcome: The failure pattern is identified before troubleshooting. If the issue is resolved by selecting the correct detector or exam setup, stop.

steps.title: Check Detector Power Status
steps.instructions: Verify that the wireless detector is powered on and has sufficient battery charge. Action: Check the detector battery indicator, detector status screen, or system detector status display. Install a charged detector battery if available. Reseat the battery fully. Expected outcome: The detector powers on and becomes available to the DRX-Revolution system. Why it matters: A low, depleted, or poorly seated detector battery is one of the most common external causes of wireless detector detection problems. If the detector connects after battery replacement or reseating, stop.

steps.title: Confirm the Correct Detector Is Being Used
steps.instructions: Make sure the detector belongs to the correct mobile system or is configured for use with that system. Action: Compare the detector asset tag, serial number, or assigned detector name with the system’s expected detector list. Check whether staff swapped detectors between rooms, departments, or mobile units. Expected outcome: The correct detector is being used with the correct DRX-Revolution unit. If the wrong detector was used and the correct detector connects, stop.

steps.title: Inspect the Detector for Physical Damage
steps.instructions: Look for obvious damage before continuing. Action: Inspect the detector housing, corners, battery compartment, contacts, handle area, and status indicators. Check for evidence of drops, fluid intrusion, cracked casing, bent contacts, or loose battery fit. Expected outcome: No visible damage is found that would explain the connection issue. If damage, fluid exposure, unusual heat, or a burnt smell is present, remove the detector from service and send it for repair evaluation.

steps.title: Reseat and Restart the Detector
steps.instructions: Power-cycle the detector if it appears frozen or unavailable. Action: Power the detector off if possible. Remove and reinstall the detector battery. Wait briefly, then power the detector back on. Return to the DRX-Revolution workstation and check detector status again. Expected outcome: The detector reinitializes and reconnects to the system. If the detector is detected after restart, stop.

steps.title: Restart the DRX-Revolution Workstation
steps.instructions: If the detector is powered and appears normal, restart the mobile unit workstation. Action: End any active exam safely. Perform a controlled system restart using the normal shutdown process. After reboot, confirm the detector status before attempting another exposure. Expected outcome: The workstation reloads detector communication services and recognizes the wireless detector. If reboot restores detector detection, stop.

steps.title: Check Wireless Environment and Distance
steps.instructions: Move the detector and mobile unit close together to rule out range or interference. Action: Place the detector near the mobile unit. Avoid testing near known wireless interference sources when possible. Try connecting in another area if the issue only occurs in one room. Expected outcome: The detector connects when close to the system and away from possible interference. Why it matters: Wireless detector communication can be affected by distance, room shielding, interference, or network congestion. If the detector connects in another location, document the location-specific behavior and escalate to imaging/network support as appropriate.

steps.title: Verify Detector Charging and Battery Contacts
steps.instructions: If the detector frequently disconnects or will not stay online, check for battery or contact issues. Action: Inspect detector battery contacts for debris, damage, or poor seating. Try a known-good charged battery. Place the suspect battery in the charger and confirm normal charger behavior. Expected outcome: A good battery powers the detector consistently and allows connection. If one battery causes the issue but another works, remove the bad battery from service.

steps.title: Test With Another Detector if Available
steps.instructions: Determine whether the problem follows the detector or stays with the mobile unit. Action: Try a known-good compatible detector with the DRX-Revolution. Try the suspect detector with its assigned compatible system if allowed by site workflow. Expected outcome: If another detector works, suspect the original detector or its battery. If no detector works, suspect the mobile unit workstation, wireless configuration, or detector communication hardware. If the issue follows one detector, remove that detector from service.

steps.title: Check Workstation Detector Selection and Exam Setup
steps.instructions: Confirm the exam workflow is not pointed to the wrong detector or inactive acquisition device. Action: Verify the correct detector is selected for the exam. Confirm the detector status shows ready or available before attempting exposure. Cancel and recreate the exam if the current study appears stuck or corrupted. Expected outcome: The selected detector matches the available wireless detector. If correcting detector selection resolves the issue, stop.

steps.title: Review Recent Changes
steps.instructions: Look for recent events that may explain the failure. Action: Ask staff whether the detector was dropped, batteries were swapped, software was updated, wireless settings changed, or the unit was moved to a new clinical area. Check whether the issue began after network maintenance or system service. Expected outcome: Recent changes are identified and documented. Why it matters: Detector communication problems often appear after battery swaps, detector swaps, software changes, or wireless/network changes.

steps.title: Perform a Basic Functional Test
steps.instructions: Once the detector is connected, confirm the system is ready before returning it to service. Action: Verify detector status shows ready. Confirm the system can complete normal detector preparation. Perform only site-approved test imaging or QA checks according to department policy. Expected outcome: The detector remains connected and the system completes the expected workflow without communication errors. If the detector disconnects again during testing, remove the device from service.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the Carestream DRX-Revolution wireless detector was not connecting and the system would not detect the detector for portable imaging.
CCR.Cause: Found detector battery low and not fully seated in the detector battery compartment during troubleshooting.
CCR.Resolution: Reseated the detector battery, installed a charged spare battery, restarted the workstation, confirmed detector status showed ready, and returned the unit to service after functional check.

helpfulDetails: Outlet tested if charging or dock power was involved
Detector battery level
Battery swapped or reseated
Detector serial number or asset tag
Whether issue followed the detector or stayed with the mobile unit
Exact detector message shown
Wireless signal or location-specific behavior
Workstation restart performed
Indicator lights on detector and battery charger
Any signs of detector damage, heat, fluid, or unusual smell
Final device status

title: Carestream DRX-Revolution Mobile X-Ray System - Battery Not Charging or Reduced Run Time
description: Battery charging issues, short run time, docking/AC power problems, charger faults, or battery-related mobile X-ray readiness concerns.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the mobile X-ray system from clinical use if battery status is low, charging is unreliable, or the unit may shut down during imaging. Action: Use another mobile X-ray unit for patient exams until the charging or run-time issue is corrected. Expected outcome: Patient imaging is not interrupted by unexpected power loss. Why it matters: A mobile X-ray system with unreliable battery operation may fail during transport, positioning, exposure setup, or image processing.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Battery Issue
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the problem is truly a battery issue or a power connection issue. Action: Ask clinical staff what they observed: Unit will not charge; Battery percentage does not increase; Unit shuts down quickly after unplugging; Unit only works while plugged in; Charging indicator does not appear; Battery drains faster than normal; Error message appears during startup or charging. Expected outcome: The reported symptom is clearly identified before replacing parts or escalating.

steps.title: Check the AC Power Source
steps.instructions: Start with the wall outlet or charging location. Action: Plug the DRX-Revolution into a known-good hospital-grade outlet. Confirm the outlet has power using an outlet tester or another known-working device if appropriate. Expected outcome: The unit recognizes AC power and begins charging. If resolved: Document the failed outlet or power source and stop troubleshooting the mobile unit.

steps.title: Inspect the Power Cord and Plug
steps.instructions: Check for external damage before assuming internal charger failure. Action: Inspect the power cord for cuts, crushed insulation, bent blades, loose strain relief, damaged plug ends, or signs of overheating. Expected outcome: The cord should be intact, secure, and free of heat damage. If damage is found: Remove the cord from use and replace it with the correct approved power cord.

steps.title: Verify the Cord Is Fully Seated
steps.instructions: A loose AC connection can mimic a battery or charger problem. Action: Confirm the power cord is fully inserted at both the wall outlet and the mobile X-ray system power inlet. Expected outcome: The charging indicator or AC power status should appear normally. If resolved: The issue was a loose or partially seated power connection.

steps.title: Check for Charging Indicators
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the system sees incoming AC power. Action: Observe the display, battery icon, power indicator, or charging status when the unit is connected to AC power. Expected outcome: The system should indicate that AC power is connected and charging is active. If no charging status appears: Continue checking external power, power cord, inlet condition, and system status.

steps.title: Inspect the AC Power Inlet Area
steps.instructions: Look for obvious external damage at the power entry point. Action: With the unit unplugged, visually inspect the AC inlet area for looseness, cracked plastic, bent contacts, debris, discoloration, or signs of overheating. Expected outcome: The inlet should be secure and undamaged. If damage, heat, or burning smell is present: Remove the unit from service and escalate for repair.

steps.title: Allow Adequate Charging Time
steps.instructions: A deeply discharged unit may need time on AC power before normal operation is restored. Action: Leave the unit connected to a verified AC source for an appropriate charging period. Monitor whether the battery percentage increases. Expected outcome: Battery level should gradually rise. If battery level does not increase: Continue troubleshooting.

steps.title: Confirm the Unit Is Not Being Used Heavily While Charging
steps.instructions: Charging may appear slow if the system is powered on, processing images, or being used repeatedly. Action: Ask whether the unit was being charged while actively used. Place the unit in a normal charging state and allow it to charge without repeated exams or movement. Expected outcome: Battery charge should increase more predictably when the system is not under active load.

steps.title: Check for Battery Age or History
steps.instructions: Reduced run time is often related to battery age, usage, or charge cycle history. Action: Review service history, prior battery replacements, repeated battery complaints, and any recent work orders for charging or run-time issues. Expected outcome: You may identify a pattern suggesting battery degradation rather than a one-time charging issue.

steps.title: Check for Environmental or Storage Issues
steps.instructions: Mobile X-ray battery performance can be affected by poor charging habits or storage conditions. Action: Ask whether the unit is regularly left unplugged, stored away from charging locations, or used heavily without full recharge cycles. Expected outcome: Operational habits may explain reduced run time. If resolved by workflow change: Educate the department to return the unit to AC power when not in use.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled Run-Time Check
steps.instructions: If the battery charges but drains quickly, verify the complaint under controlled conditions. Action: Fully charge the unit, disconnect from AC power, and observe battery percentage drop during normal idle and operational use. Expected outcome: Battery level should decline at a reasonable rate. If the battery drops rapidly: Battery degradation, internal power draw, or charging system issues are likely.

steps.title: Restart the System if Charging Status Appears Frozen
steps.instructions: A software status display issue may make charging appear abnormal. Action: Safely power down and restart the unit while connected to verified AC power. Expected outcome: Battery and AC power status should refresh normally. If resolved: Monitor the unit and document that the charging display recovered after restart.

steps.title: Check for Related System Errors
steps.instructions: Battery and charging problems may appear with additional system alerts. Action: Look for battery, charger, AC input, power supply, thermal, or system communication messages on the display. Expected outcome: Any related messages should be documented exactly as shown. If errors are present: Include the exact wording in the work order and escalate if the error persists.

steps.title: Verify Final Device Readiness
steps.instructions: Before returning the unit to service, confirm it can charge and operate reliably. Action: Confirm: AC power is detected; Battery level increases while charging; Unit remains powered on when unplugged; Battery run time is acceptable for clinical use; No charging or power errors remain; No unusual heat, smell, or noise is present. Expected outcome: The DRX-Revolution is safe to return to clinical use.

CCR.Complaint: Radiology reported that the Carestream DRX-Revolution would not hold a charge and had reduced run time during portable exams.
CCR.Cause: Verified AC outlet and power cord were good, but the unit charged slowly and battery level dropped rapidly after disconnecting from AC power.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the DRX-Revolution from service, labeled it Out of Service, documented battery behavior, and sent the unit for bench evaluation of the battery and charging system.

helpfulDetails: Outlet tested and confirmed good
Power cord inspected or swapped
Charging indicator present or absent
Battery percentage before and after charging
Approximate run time after full charge
Whether unit works on AC power
Whether unit shuts down when unplugged
Any battery, charger, or power error messages
AC inlet condition
Unusual heat, smell, or sound
Final device status

title: Carestream DRX-Revolution Mobile X-Ray System - Drive handle, brake, or motor assist fault
description: Drive handle, brake, or motor assist problems that affect safe movement, parking, steering, or powered transport of the DRX-Revolution.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the mobile X-ray system from patient use if movement, braking, or steering control is unreliable. Action: Move the unit away from patients, beds, stretchers, staff, and wall-mounted equipment if it can be moved safely. If the unit is in a patient care area and cannot be moved safely, secure the area and notify clinical staff. Do not continue transporting the unit if the brake does not hold, the drive assist surges, or the handle response is unpredictable. Expected outcome: The device is no longer creating a movement or collision risk. Why it matters: A mobile X-ray system is heavy. Brake or motor assist problems can cause injury, equipment damage, or unsafe bedside positioning.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Problem
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact behavior before assuming a drive system failure. Action: Ask clinical staff what happened: Did the unit fail to move? Did it move slowly? Did it lurch or surge? Did the brake stay engaged? Did the brake fail to hold? Did the drive handle feel loose, stuck, or unresponsive? Did the issue occur after charging, after transport, or after hitting an obstacle? Expected outcome: The failure mode is clearly identified and documented. If the unit is unsafe to move, stop troubleshooting and remove it from service.

steps.title: Check for Obvious Physical Obstruction
steps.instructions: Inspect the wheels and base for anything blocking normal movement. Action: Look for debris, cords, linen, tape, packaging, floor mats, cable wraps, or foreign objects caught around the drive wheels or casters. Check that the unit is not against a wall, bed, threshold, elevator gap, or uneven floor surface. Expected outcome: Wheels and casters are clear and able to rotate freely. If removing an obstruction restores normal movement and braking, return the unit to service after a functional check and document the correction.

steps.title: Verify the Unit Is Powered On and Fully Awake
steps.instructions: The drive assist may not respond normally if the system is off, asleep, booting, or in a restricted state. Action: Confirm the DRX-Revolution is powered on. Allow the system to complete startup. Check for active system messages, fault indicators, or prompts that may prevent movement. Expected outcome: The unit is fully powered and ready for controlled movement. If normal drive operation returns after startup completes, document the behavior and return the unit to service if no fault repeats.

steps.title: Check Battery Charge Level
steps.instructions: Low battery condition may reduce motor assist performance or prevent normal powered movement. Action: Check the battery charge indicator. Connect the unit to a known-good AC outlet if charge is low. Allow the unit to charge long enough to confirm whether the drive function improves. Expected outcome: Battery level is adequate for drive assist operation. If the drive issue only occurs at low battery and resolves after charging, document the battery level and charging result.

steps.title: Confirm the Unit Is Properly Unplugged Before Transport
steps.instructions: Some mobile systems may restrict drive operation when connected to AC power or when the power cord is not properly stowed. Action: Confirm the AC cord is disconnected before attempting to drive the unit. Inspect the cord reel or cord storage area. Verify the power cord is not dragging, snagged, or caught under the base. Expected outcome: The unit is free for safe movement and the power cord is not interfering with transport. If the issue was caused by the cord being connected or caught, correct it, test movement, and stop.

steps.title: Inspect the Drive Handle for Damage or Binding
steps.instructions: The drive handle is a common user interface point for motor assist problems. Action: Inspect the handle for cracks, looseness, fluid residue, sticky movement, bent components, or physical damage. Gently move the handle through its normal range without forcing it. Check whether the handle returns to neutral correctly. Expected outcome: The handle moves smoothly and returns to neutral without sticking. If the handle is physically damaged, sticky, loose, or does not return properly, remove the unit from service for repair.

steps.title: Check for Brake Release Problems
steps.instructions: A brake that remains engaged can feel like drive motor failure. Action: Attempt to move the unit only in a safe, open area. Listen and feel for whether the brake releases when the drive handle is activated. Check whether the unit resists movement even when drive assist should be active. Expected outcome: Brake releases smoothly and the unit moves predictably. If the brake does not release, releases intermittently, or drags, remove the unit from service.

steps.title: Check for Brake Holding Problems
steps.instructions: A brake that does not hold creates a serious safety hazard. Action: Park the unit on a flat surface. Confirm whether it remains stationary when the drive handle is released. Do not test on a slope or near patients. Expected outcome: The unit remains stopped when no drive command is applied. If the unit rolls, creeps, or does not hold position, remove it from service immediately.

steps.title: Check for Drive Assist Direction and Response
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the motor assist responds correctly to forward and reverse commands. Action: In a clear area, slowly test forward and reverse movement. Check for delayed response, no response, jerky movement, unexpected acceleration, grinding noise, or uneven drive. Expected outcome: Drive assist responds smoothly and predictably in both directions. If the unit surges, hesitates, moves unexpectedly, or only drives in one direction, stop testing and remove it from service.

steps.title: Inspect Drive Wheels and Casters
steps.instructions: Damaged wheels can mimic motor or brake faults. Action: Inspect drive wheels and casters for flat spots, missing tread, loose hardware, cracks, contamination, or material wrapped around the axle area. Check whether any wheel appears locked, dragging, tilted, or misaligned. Expected outcome: Wheels and casters are intact, clean, and rotating normally. If a wheel or caster is damaged or binding, remove the unit from service for repair.

steps.title: Check Floor and Environmental Conditions
steps.instructions: The issue may be caused by the surface rather than the drive system. Action: Check whether the problem occurs only on carpet, wet floors, thresholds, elevator gaps, ramps, damaged flooring, or high-friction surfaces. Move the unit to a flat, dry, open floor area if safe. Expected outcome: Drive behavior is consistent on a normal hospital floor surface. If the unit only struggles on a difficult surface but operates normally elsewhere, document the condition and educate staff as appropriate.

steps.title: Check for Recent Impact or Collision
steps.instructions: Drive faults may appear after the base, handle, or wheel area is hit. Action: Inspect the base, bumpers, drive handle, column area, and wheel area for signs of impact. Ask whether the unit recently hit a doorway, elevator, bed, wall, or threshold. Expected outcome: No obvious impact damage is present. If impact damage is found, remove the unit from service for inspection and repair.

steps.title: Power Cycle the System Only When Safe
steps.instructions: A temporary control issue may clear after a proper restart, but this should not be used to ignore recurring faults. Action: Park the unit safely. Power the system down using normal shutdown. Wait briefly, then power it back on. Check whether the same drive, brake, or motor assist fault returns. Expected outcome: The system restarts normally and drive assist works without repeated fault messages. If the fault returns, do not keep power cycling. Remove the unit from service.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled Functional Check
steps.instructions: Before returning the system to clinical use, verify safe transport behavior. Action: In a clear area, confirm: Forward drive works. Reverse drive works. The handle returns to neutral. Braking engages when the handle is released. The unit does not roll when stopped. There are no unusual noises, smells, heat, or warning messages. Expected outcome: The unit drives, stops, and parks safely. If any part of the movement or braking function remains questionable, remove the system from service.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the Carestream DRX-Revolution would not drive normally and the brake felt like it was staying engaged during transport.
CCR.Cause: Checked battery level, AC cord status, wheels, floor condition, and handle movement; no external obstruction was found, and the brake continued to drag during controlled testing.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the DRX-Revolution from service, labeled it Out of Service, and sent it for bench evaluation of the brake and motor assist system.

helpfulDetails: Outlet tested if charging was involved.
Battery level at time of failure.
Whether the unit was connected to AC power.
Whether the power cord was fully stowed.
Drive handle condition.
Forward and reverse drive behavior.
Brake release behavior.
Brake holding behavior.
Wheel or caster condition.
Any obstruction found around wheels.
Floor condition where the issue occurred.
Any warning messages or fault indicators.
Unusual noises, vibration, heat, or smell.
Evidence of impact or collision.
Final device status.

title: Carestream DRX-Revolution Mobile X-Ray System - Image not sending to PACS or worklist not updating
description: Troubleshooting PACS send failures, missing worklist updates, network issues, wireless connection problems, and image transfer delays on a DRX-Revolution.

steps.title: 1. Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot image transfer or worklist issues during an active patient exam unless the clinical workflow is already complete. Action: Confirm the exposure is complete and the patient is no longer dependent on the mobile X-ray unit. If another urgent portable exam is needed, use a known-working mobile X-ray system. Do not delete images, repeat exposures, or reboot the system until image status is understood. Expected outcome: Patient care continues safely, and completed images are protected from accidental loss. Why it matters: PACS and worklist issues are usually workflow or connectivity problems, but mishandling stored images can cause repeat imaging or documentation problems.

steps.title: 2. Verify the Reported Problem
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the issue is with image sending, worklist updates, or both. Action: Ask the technologist what specifically failed: Images not sending to PACS. Images stuck in queue. Worklist not refreshing. Patient not appearing on worklist. Images sent but not visible in PACS. Error only occurs in one location. Error only occurs with one accession or patient. Check the DRX-Revolution display for network, DICOM, PACS, worklist, or send queue messages. Expected outcome: The failure type is clearly identified before troubleshooting. If the issue is only one patient or accession, focus on order/worklist data first. If all exams fail, focus on network or DICOM connectivity.

steps.title: 3. Confirm the Exam Was Completed Correctly
steps.instructions: Make sure the image is available and ready to send. Action: Check that the exam is closed, completed, or in the proper status for transmission. Verify the image is not still pending review, rejected, held, or unsent. Check whether the image appears in the local study list or image queue. Expected outcome: The image should be present locally and available for DICOM transfer. If the image was never completed or is still held locally, complete the exam workflow and attempt to resend. If successful, stop.

steps.title: 4. Check the Send Queue
steps.instructions: Look for images stuck in the transmission queue. Action: Open the local send queue, network queue, or image transmission status area. Look for: Pending images. Failed images. Repeated retry attempts. Destination errors. Association errors. Timeout messages. Storage commitment issues, if used. Attempt a manual resend if the image is safely stored and the system allows it. Expected outcome: Images should either send successfully or produce a clear error that helps identify the cause. If manual resend works, document the temporary transfer delay and stop.

steps.title: 5. Verify Wireless Network Connection
steps.instructions: Mobile X-ray systems commonly fail to send images when wireless coverage is weak or disconnected. Action: Check the wireless connection icon or network status on the DRX-Revolution. Confirm the unit is connected to the correct hospital wireless network. Move the unit to an area with known good wireless coverage, such as near the department, a main corridor, or another location where mobile imaging normally sends successfully. Avoid testing in elevators, shielded rooms, basements, stairwells, or dead zones. Expected outcome: The unit should show a stable network connection. If the unit reconnects and images send successfully, the likely cause was wireless coverage or roaming. Stop and document the location.

steps.title: 6. Check Whether the Issue Is Location-Specific
steps.instructions: Determine if the problem follows the unit or the wireless area. Action: Ask whether other mobile X-ray systems can send from the same location. If possible, compare behavior with another known-working portable unit. Test the affected DRX-Revolution from a location where wireless image transfer normally works. Expected outcome: The issue should be narrowed to the unit, network area, or hospital wireless infrastructure. If multiple units fail in the same location, escalate to IT/network support rather than replacing imaging hardware.

steps.title: 7. Confirm Network Login or Authentication Status
steps.instructions: Some systems may appear powered on but not fully authenticated to the hospital network. Action: Check whether the unit requires user login, network authentication, domain access, or wireless certificate validation. Confirm the operator is logged in correctly. Look for warnings related to expired credentials, failed authentication, locked account, or disconnected network services. Expected outcome: The unit should be fully logged into the system and connected to hospital network resources. If login or authentication restores PACS/worklist function, stop and document the access issue.

steps.title: 8. Verify Worklist Search Criteria
steps.instructions: A worklist may fail to update because the search filters do not match the expected order. Action: Check the worklist query fields, including: Date. Modality. Location. Patient name. Accession number. Scheduled station. Department. Exam status. Clear unnecessary filters and refresh the worklist. Search by accession number if available. Expected outcome: The correct order should appear if the worklist server is reachable and the query matches the order. If the order appears after changing filters, the device is likely functioning normally. Stop and document the filter/search issue.

steps.title: 9. Confirm the Order Exists in RIS/EMR
steps.instructions: Do not assume the DRX-Revolution is failing if the order was not placed or released correctly. Action: Ask imaging staff or the modality lead to confirm the order exists in the RIS/EMR. Verify the order is active, released, scheduled, and assigned correctly. Confirm that the patient identifiers and accession number match what the technologist expects. Expected outcome: A valid order should exist before the worklist can retrieve it. If the order is missing or incorrect in the RIS/EMR, the issue is not a device failure. Stop and document that the order/worklist source requires correction.

steps.title: 10. Check PACS and Worklist Destination Availability
steps.instructions: The device may be working, but the receiving system may be down or unavailable. Action: Ask whether other imaging systems are sending to PACS normally. Check with radiology IT, PACS support, or the imaging supervisor for known outages. Determine whether PACS, RIS, worklist broker, DICOM router, or network services are experiencing downtime. Expected outcome: Hospital imaging systems should confirm whether this is isolated to the DRX-Revolution or part of a larger outage. If there is a known PACS/worklist outage, stop device troubleshooting and document the external system issue.

steps.title: 11. Verify Correct PACS Destination Selection
steps.instructions: Some systems may have multiple send destinations. Action: Confirm the technologist selected the correct PACS, archive, router, or department destination. Check whether the image was accidentally sent to a test destination, alternate archive, local storage, or incorrect facility. Review recent send history if available. Expected outcome: Images should be sent to the intended production PACS destination. If the wrong destination was selected, resend to the correct destination and stop if successful.

steps.title: 12. Confirm Date and Time Accuracy
steps.instructions: Incorrect system date or time can affect worklist queries, image timestamps, and DICOM routing. Action: Check the DRX-Revolution date and time. Compare it with hospital network time. Look for incorrect time zone, wrong date, or large time drift. Expected outcome: The unit should match hospital time closely. If the date/time is incorrect and correcting it restores worklist or PACS behavior, stop and document the finding.

steps.title: 13. Reboot the System Only After Image Status Is Known
steps.instructions: A reboot may restore network services, but it should not be the first action if unsent images are present. Action: Confirm any unsent images are safely stored locally. Notify the technologist before rebooting. Perform a normal shutdown and restart of the DRX-Revolution. After restart, verify network connection, refresh the worklist, and attempt resend. Expected outcome: Temporary software or network service issues may clear after reboot. If reboot restores PACS or worklist function, document the fault as intermittent communication/software state issue and stop.

steps.title: 14. Check for Physical Network Options if Available
steps.instructions: If the unit supports wired network connection or docking, use it to separate wireless issues from DICOM configuration problems. Action: Connect the unit to an approved hospital network port or docking area if available. Confirm network link lights or connection status. Attempt worklist refresh and image resend. Expected outcome: If wired network works but wireless does not, the issue is likely wireless connectivity, configuration, antenna, or network coverage related. If wired transfer works, document the wireless-specific failure and escalate to the appropriate support group.

steps.title: 15. Review Error Messages Before Escalation
steps.instructions: Capture exact communication errors before sending the device for repair. Action: Record any displayed messages related to: DICOM association failure. PACS connection timeout. Worklist query failure. Network unavailable. Destination unreachable. Authentication failure. Storage failed. Queue retry failure. Take a photo of the error screen if allowed by facility policy and no patient information is exposed. Expected outcome: The repair team, PACS team, or vendor has useful information for diagnosis.

CCR.Complaint: Technologist reported that the Carestream DRX-Revolution completed portable chest images but the study would not send to PACS and the worklist would not refresh.
CCR.Cause: Found the unit connected to weak wireless coverage in a patient care area, with images pending in the send queue and worklist query timing out.
CCR.Resolution: Moved unit to known-good wireless area, confirmed network reconnection, manually resent pending images successfully, refreshed worklist, and returned unit to service.

helpfulDetails: Whether images were stored locally
Whether images were stuck in the send queue
Exact PACS or worklist error message
Whether the issue affected one patient or all studies
Wireless signal status
Location where the problem occurred
Whether other mobile units had the same issue
Whether PACS/RIS downtime was reported
Destination selected for image send
Whether manual resend was successful
Whether reboot was performed
Whether wired network testing was available
Final device status

title: Carestream DRX-Revolution Mobile X-Ray System - Exposure Inhibited Due to Generator, Tube, or System Interlock Fault
description: Troubleshooting guide for exposure inhibited conditions caused by readiness, generator, tube, battery, detector, door, motion, or safety interlock problems on a Carestream DRX-Revolution Mobile X-Ray System.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not continue attempting exposures on a patient until the inhibit condition is understood. Remove the DRX-Revolution from patient use if imaging cannot be completed safely. Use another mobile X-ray system if the exam is urgent. Do not repeatedly attempt exposures if the unit displays generator, tube, or system fault messages. Expected outcome: Patient imaging is completed with a reliable system, and the affected unit is evaluated safely. Why it matters: Exposure inhibit faults may prevent image acquisition, interrupt patient care, or indicate a safety condition that the system is intentionally blocking.

steps.title: Verify the Exact Inhibit Message
steps.instructions: Confirm what the system is actually reporting before troubleshooting. Check the workstation display, system status area, generator status, and any warning or error messages. Document whether the issue appears as exposure inhibited. Document whether the issue appears as generator not ready. Document whether the issue appears as tube not ready. Document whether the issue appears as system interlock active. Document whether the issue appears as detector not ready. Document whether the issue appears as battery or power-related inhibit. Document whether the issue appears as emergency stop or safety interlock condition. Expected outcome: The exact symptom is identified instead of treating all exposure failures as the same issue. If the issue clears after acknowledging a temporary message and the unit exposes normally, document the event and stop.

steps.title: Confirm the Exam and Exposure Workflow
steps.instructions: Make sure the unit is in a valid state to allow exposure. Confirm a patient or test exam is selected. Verify the correct anatomy, view, detector selection, and exposure technique are selected. Confirm the system is not waiting for operator input, image acceptance, detector selection, or procedure confirmation. Exit and re-enter the exam if the software appears stuck. Expected outcome: The system allows exposure once the workflow is complete and no required selection is missing. Why it matters: Mobile X-ray systems may inhibit exposure when the workstation, detector, or generator workflow is incomplete.

steps.title: Check Emergency Stop and Safety Controls
steps.instructions: A safety control may be active or partially engaged. Inspect the emergency stop button and confirm it is not pressed. Reset the emergency stop if it is engaged. Check for any visible safety switch, brake, drive, or system lockout condition. Confirm the unit is stationary and stable before attempting exposure. Expected outcome: Exposure is enabled if the inhibit was caused by an active safety stop or motion-related interlock. If the emergency stop will not reset or the system continues to show a safety interlock fault, remove the unit from service.

steps.title: Verify Power and Battery Status
steps.instructions: Low battery or unstable power can prevent generator readiness. Check battery charge level. Connect the unit to a known-good AC outlet if battery charge is low. Confirm the charging indicator behaves normally. Allow the system time to stabilize after connecting to AC power. Avoid attempting exposure during a low-battery or shutdown warning condition. Expected outcome: The generator becomes ready once adequate power is available. Why it matters: A mobile X-ray generator requires enough stored energy to support exposure. Low or unstable battery conditions can trigger exposure inhibit messages.

steps.title: Confirm the Unit Is Fully Booted and Ready
steps.instructions: The generator, workstation, and detector must all be ready before exposure. Restart the system if it appears partially booted, frozen, or stuck in a not-ready state. Allow the DRX-Revolution to complete startup fully. Check that the workstation is responsive. Confirm the generator status changes to ready before attempting exposure. Expected outcome: A normal restart clears temporary software or communication readiness issues. If the same inhibit returns immediately after restart, continue troubleshooting.

steps.title: Check Detector Readiness
steps.instructions: A detector or detector communication issue may prevent exposure. Confirm the wireless detector is powered on. Check detector battery level. Verify the correct detector is selected for the exam. Confirm the detector is connected or recognized by the system. Reseat or reconnect the detector battery if appropriate and safe. Try a known-good compatible detector if available. Expected outcome: Exposure is allowed once the selected detector is available and ready. Why it matters: The system may inhibit exposure if it cannot confirm that the intended image receptor is ready.

steps.title: Inspect the Exposure Switch
steps.instructions: The exposure switch may be disconnected, damaged, or not completing the prep/expose sequence. Inspect the hand switch and cable for visible damage. Confirm the switch is fully connected. Check whether the first-stage prep and second-stage exposure actions feel normal. Try a known-good compatible exposure switch if available. Expected outcome: The unit allows prep and exposure if the original switch or connection was the cause. If the switch is damaged, remove it from use and replace it according to site policy.

steps.title: Verify Tube Head and Arm Positioning
steps.instructions: The system may block exposure if positioning, locks, or tube readiness are abnormal. Confirm the tube head, column, and arm are positioned normally. Check whether any mechanical locks, brakes, or detents are not fully seated. Look for messages related to arm position, tube position, SID, or motion. Reposition the tube head and arm, then attempt a test exposure in a safe test environment. Expected outcome: Exposure is enabled if the inhibit was caused by an unresolved positioning or motion interlock. Why it matters: Mobile X-ray systems may use interlocks to prevent exposure while the system is moving, unstable, or not in a valid imaging position.

steps.title: Check Technique Settings
steps.instructions: Invalid or extreme technique settings can prevent exposure. Review kVp, mAs, exposure mode, and selected exam protocol. Select a standard adult chest or general test protocol if permitted by local policy. Avoid unusual manual settings during troubleshooting. Confirm the selected technique is within normal operating range. Expected outcome: Exposure is allowed if the inhibit was caused by an invalid or unsupported technique selection.

steps.title: Check for Tube Heat or Cooling Delay
steps.instructions: The tube may be temporarily inhibited due to heat or readiness timing. Look for tube heat, cooling, or wait messages. Ask clinical staff whether multiple exposures were attempted in rapid sequence. Allow the unit to cool if a heat-related message is present. Do not bypass or ignore tube heat warnings. Expected outcome: Exposure becomes available after the tube returns to an allowable operating condition. Why it matters: Tube heat protection prevents damage to the tube and protects against unsafe operating conditions.

steps.title: Check for Recent Collisions, Drops, or Movement Issues
steps.instructions: Physical impact or motion faults may create system interlocks. Ask whether the unit recently hit a wall, door frame, bed, elevator threshold, or other obstruction. Inspect the tube head, arm, detector, exposure switch, and workstation for obvious damage. Check for unusual sounds, binding, loose covers, or warning messages. Expected outcome: External damage or impact history is identified before assuming an internal generator failure. If impact damage is suspected, remove the unit from service for inspection.

steps.title: Perform a Safe Test Exposure Only If Appropriate
steps.instructions: Test only when the area is controlled and radiation safety requirements are met. Use the site-approved test process, phantom, or non-clinical test setup. Follow radiation safety practices. Verify whether prep occurs, whether exposure is attempted, and whether the inhibit appears before or during exposure. Do not repeatedly force exposures after a generator or tube fault. Expected outcome: The failure point is confirmed: before prep, during prep, at exposure command, or after exposure attempt.

steps.title: Review Error History or Event Logs If Available
steps.instructions: The system may contain useful fault history. Check accessible system messages or event history if available to Clinical Engineering. Record exact fault wording, fault codes, timestamps, and conditions. Do not clear logs before documenting useful information. Expected outcome: Repair evaluation has enough information to separate generator, tube, detector, battery, or interlock faults.

CCR.Complaint: Radiology reported the Carestream DRX-Revolution would not expose and displayed an exposure inhibited message during a portable exam.
CCR.Cause: Clinical Engineering found the unit powered on normally, but exposure remained inhibited after checking AC power, battery status, detector readiness, exposure switch connection, positioning, and workflow setup.
CCR.Resolution: Unit was removed from service, labeled Out of Service, fault details were documented, and the system was escalated for generator/tube/interlock service evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Alarm behavior, including the exact exposure inhibit message or fault code
Accessories swapped, including detector, detector battery, or exposure switch
Power behavior, including outlet tested, AC power verified, battery charge level, and charging behavior
Environmental factors, including whether the issue followed a collision or transport event
Indicator lights, including charging indicators and system readiness indicators
Final device status, including whether the unit was returned to service or labeled Out of Service

title: Carestream DRX-Evolution Radiographic System - DRX Detector Not Connecting, Charging, or Pairing
description: DRX detector connection, charging, pairing, or recognition problems caused by power, dock, battery, wireless, or configuration issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot the DRX detector during an active patient exam if imaging cannot be completed safely. Remove the room from clinical use if the detector is unavailable, intermittently dropping connection, or not charging. Direct staff to another radiographic room or backup detector if patient imaging is urgent. Do not continue repeated exposure attempts if the detector is not properly recognized. Expected outcome: Patient care continues using a reliable imaging setup before troubleshooting begins. Why it matters: A detector communication or charging issue can delay imaging, cause repeat exams, or interrupt urgent care.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Problem
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the issue is connection, charging, pairing, or image acquisition related. Ask staff what they observed: Detector not detected by the system, Detector will not pair, Detector battery not charging, Detector connects but drops offline, Detector appears charged but is not available for imaging. Check the workstation or console for detector status messages. Expected outcome: The failure type is clearly identified before replacing parts or escalating. If the detector is now recognized and ready for use, document the condition and stop.

steps.title: Confirm the Correct Detector Is Being Used
steps.instructions: Verify that the detector belongs to the DRX-Evolution room or is assigned for use with that system. Check the detector ID, room assignment, or asset tag. Confirm staff did not bring in a detector from another room or portable system. Compare the detector shown on the console with the physical detector being used. Expected outcome: The system is attempting to connect to the correct detector. Why it matters: DRX detectors may look similar, but the system may only recognize detectors configured or paired to that room. If the correct detector is selected and the system connects normally, stop.

steps.title: Check Detector Battery Status
steps.instructions: Confirm the detector has enough battery charge to power on and communicate. Check the detector battery indicator if available. Place the detector into the approved charger or docking location. Allow the detector to sit long enough to show charging activity. If a spare compatible battery is available and approved for that detector, swap batteries and retest. Expected outcome: The detector shows charge activity and powers on normally. If the detector powers on and reconnects after charging or battery replacement, document the issue and stop.

steps.title: Inspect the Detector Charging Dock or Charger
steps.instructions: Check for simple charging failures before assuming the detector battery is bad. Confirm the charger or dock is plugged into a working outlet. Check for charger indicator lights. Inspect the dock contacts for debris, fluid, damage, bent pins, or poor alignment. Reseat the detector or battery fully in the charger. Test the outlet with another device or outlet tester if appropriate. Expected outcome: The charger powers normally and the detector or battery makes solid contact. If charging resumes after reseating, cleaning external contacts, or changing outlets, stop.

steps.title: Inspect the Detector Externally
steps.instructions: Look for physical damage that may prevent charging, pairing, or wireless communication. Inspect the detector housing for cracks, swelling, loose covers, fluid intrusion, damaged contacts, or missing labels. Check for signs of impact or being dropped. Smell for burning or unusual electrical odor. Feel for abnormal heat. Expected outcome: No visible damage, overheating, fluid exposure, or unsafe condition is present. If damage, heat, smell, or fluid intrusion is found, remove the detector from service and do not continue troubleshooting.

steps.title: Restart the Detector and Imaging Workstation
steps.instructions: Clear temporary communication or software lockup conditions. Power the detector off if possible. Restart the DRX-Evolution workstation or acquisition console according to site procedure. Power the detector back on after the system is fully available. Attempt detector connection again. Expected outcome: The detector reconnects and appears available for imaging. If the detector reconnects after restart, document the reset and return the system to service after a functional check.

steps.title: Check Wireless Network or Detector Communication Status
steps.instructions: Determine whether the detector is failing because of a wireless communication problem. Confirm the room’s wireless detector communication hardware is powered. Check for disconnected network cables, loose access point power, or damaged antenna hardware if externally visible. Look for recent network, room, or IT changes. Confirm other room network functions are working, such as worklist or PACS connectivity, if applicable. Expected outcome: The room network and detector communication path appear active. Why it matters: A detector may be functional but unavailable if the wireless path or room network is down. If a room network issue is found, escalate to the appropriate imaging IT, network, or service support team.

steps.title: Attempt Detector Reselection or Re-Pairing Only If Site-Approved
steps.instructions: Use only normal operator-level or Clinical Engineering-approved pairing steps. Open the detector management or detector selection screen if accessible. Confirm the detector is listed. Select the correct detector if it is available but not active. Attempt pairing only if your site allows Clinical Engineering to perform this function without vendor service access. Expected outcome: The detector pairs or becomes available for imaging. Do not force service-level pairing, configuration changes, or calibration steps unless trained and authorized. If the detector pairs successfully and passes a test image or room readiness check, stop.

steps.title: Test With a Known-Good Compatible Detector or Battery If Available
steps.instructions: Isolate whether the problem follows the detector, battery, charger, or room. Place a known-good compatible detector into the room workflow if your site allows detector sharing. Test the suspect detector in an approved charger or compatible room if permitted. Swap only approved batteries, chargers, or accessories. Expected outcome: If the known-good detector works, the issue likely follows the original detector or its battery. If no detector connects, the issue may be with the room system, wireless communication, or configuration. If the suspect detector fails everywhere, the detector likely requires repair. If the issue is isolated clearly, document the result and escalate the failing component.

steps.title: Check for Recent Drops, Fluid Exposure, or Cleaning Issues
steps.instructions: Ask staff whether the detector was recently dropped, cleaned heavily, or exposed to fluid. Inspect the detector bag, cover, charging contacts, and seams. Check whether disinfectant residue or moisture is present near contacts or battery areas. Confirm the detector was allowed to dry before charging or use. Expected outcome: No evidence of fluid contamination or handling damage is present. If fluid exposure or impact damage is suspected, remove the detector from service for repair evaluation.

steps.title: Perform a Safe Functional Check
steps.instructions: After correction, confirm the system is ready before returning it to clinical use. Verify the detector shows connected or ready status. Confirm battery level is acceptable. Confirm the detector remains connected for several minutes. Acquire a test image only if allowed by site procedure and radiation safety policy. Confirm images transfer normally to the workstation. Expected outcome: The DRX-Evolution recognizes the detector, detector status remains stable, and image acquisition is functional. If the detector passes the functional check, return the system to service.

CCR.Complaint: Radiology reported the Carestream DRX-Evolution detector would not connect to the room and was not available for imaging.
CCR.Cause: Clinical Engineering found the detector battery was fully discharged and the charging dock was not powered due to a loose power connection.
CCR.Resolution: Reseated the charger power cord, confirmed charging indicator activity, allowed the detector to charge, verified detector connection at the workstation, and returned the room to service.

helpfulDetails: Outlet tested
Charger or dock indicator light status
Detector battery level
Detector ID or asset tag
Whether the detector was recognized by the workstation
Whether pairing was attempted
Any wireless or network messages
Whether another detector or battery was tested
Signs of damage, fluid exposure, unusual heat, or odor
Final detector and room status

title: Carestream DRX-Evolution Radiographic System - Image Acquisition Failure or Exam Not Completing
description: Image acquisition failure, incomplete exams, detector communication issues, workstation problems, or workflow interruptions on a Carestream DRX-Evolution system.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot the DRX-Evolution while the patient is positioned for an active exam unless the issue is minor and does not delay care. Remove the patient from the imaging position if the exam cannot continue safely. Notify clinical staff that the room is experiencing an acquisition or workflow issue. Use another radiographic room or portable system if imaging is urgent. Expected outcome: Patient care continues without relying on a system that may not complete the exam. Why it matters: Image acquisition failures can delay diagnosis, increase repeat positioning, or lead to unnecessary repeat exposure attempts.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Failure
steps.instructions: Confirm exactly where the exam is failing. Ask the technologist what happened: Was the exposure not captured? Did the image fail to appear? Did the exam freeze before completion? Did the detector fail to connect? Did the image acquire but not save? Did the study fail to close or send? Expected outcome: The failure point is identified before replacing accessories or escalating. If the issue is resolved after correcting workflow or restarting the exam, stop.

steps.title: Check for Active Error Messages or System Prompts
steps.instructions: Look for visible messages on the acquisition workstation, generator console, detector status area, or room control interface. Record the exact error message. Check whether the system is asking for detector selection, patient/exam confirmation, room selection, cassette registration, or exposure readiness. Expected outcome: Any displayed fault is documented and used to guide the next step. Why it matters: Many image acquisition failures are caused by incomplete workflow steps rather than hardware failure.

steps.title: Confirm the Correct Patient and Exam Are Selected
steps.instructions: Verify that the correct patient, accession number, body part, projection, and detector are selected before exposure. Confirm the exam is open and active on the acquisition workstation. Confirm the correct image receptor/detector is assigned. Confirm the projection is selected and ready for acquisition. Expected outcome: The system is ready to receive the exposure under the correct exam. If the technologist had the wrong exam, projection, or detector selected and correcting it allows acquisition, stop.

steps.title: Check Detector Status and Connection
steps.instructions: Verify that the DRX detector is available, powered, connected, and assigned to the room. Check detector battery level. Confirm detector wireless or wired connection status. Verify the detector is not assigned to another room, portable system, or exam. Remove and reinsert the detector battery if approved by department workflow. Place the detector in its charger or dock briefly if the battery or connection status is questionable. Expected outcome: Detector status changes to ready, connected, or available. If the detector reconnects and the exam completes normally, stop.

steps.title: Inspect Detector Battery and Charging Contacts
steps.instructions: Check for a low battery, loose battery, damaged battery latch, or dirty/damaged charging contacts. Swap with a known-good charged detector battery if available. Inspect battery seating. Confirm the detector powers on normally. Check for obvious physical damage, swelling, cracked housing, or liquid intrusion. Expected outcome: Detector power is stable and the detector remains connected during acquisition. If a battery swap resolves the issue, document the failed battery and remove it from use.

steps.title: Check Room Network and Workstation Connectivity
steps.instructions: Confirm the acquisition workstation has network communication and is not disconnected from required hospital or imaging systems. Check network cable connections at the workstation if accessible. Verify link lights at the network jack or switch connection if visible. Confirm other network-dependent functions are working, such as worklist refresh or PACS destination availability. Expected outcome: The workstation has stable network communication. Why it matters: Some exams may acquire locally but fail to complete, close, or transfer if network communication is interrupted.

steps.title: Restart the Acquisition Application
steps.instructions: If the workstation appears frozen, delayed, or stuck in the exam workflow, restart the acquisition software if safe and allowed by site procedure. Close the exam if possible. Exit and restart the acquisition application. Reopen the patient exam and attempt a test workflow without exposing a patient. Expected outcome: The software responds normally and allows the exam workflow to continue. If the application restart resolves the issue, document the software lockup and final status.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Workstation and Room System
steps.instructions: If the system remains locked, not ready, or unable to complete acquisition, perform a controlled restart. Confirm no patient is being imaged. Shut down the acquisition workstation properly. Power cycle the workstation and applicable room controls according to local procedure. Allow all components to fully boot and reconnect. Expected outcome: The workstation, detector, generator, and room system return to normal ready status. If the system completes a test acquisition after restart, stop.

steps.title: Verify Generator and Exposure Readiness
steps.instructions: Check whether the acquisition failure is actually caused by the generator not completing exposure. Confirm the generator is ready. Check for exposure inhibit messages. Verify tube stand, wall stand, table bucky, grid, and detector selection match the requested exam. Confirm no emergency stop, door interlock, tube lock, or positioning interlock is active. Expected outcome: The system reaches exposure-ready status before image acquisition is attempted. Why it matters: If the generator never completes exposure, the workstation may appear to have an image acquisition problem even though the exposure was inhibited.

steps.title: Check Bucky, Table, Wall Stand, and Detector Selection
steps.instructions: Confirm the selected receptor matches the physical imaging setup. Verify whether the exam is using the table bucky, wall bucky, or wireless detector. Confirm the detector is properly seated if using a bucky tray. Check that the selected receptor on the workstation matches the detector position. Expected outcome: The system is not waiting for a different image receptor than the one being used. If correcting receptor selection resolves the failed acquisition, stop.

steps.title: Perform a Non-Patient Test Image if Permitted
steps.instructions: After patient safety is addressed, perform a controlled test exposure using site-approved phantom or QA process. Use a test patient or QA workflow if allowed by facility policy. Confirm the detector captures the image. Confirm the image displays on the workstation. Confirm the exam can be completed, saved, and sent if needed. Expected outcome: The acquisition chain is verified from exposure through image display and completion. Do not perform unnecessary radiation exposures outside approved testing procedures.

steps.title: Check for Repeat Failure Pattern
steps.instructions: Determine whether the issue is isolated or repeatable. Confirm whether the problem occurs with: One detector only. One room position only. One exam type only. One technologist login only. All exams. All detectors. Expected outcome: The failure pattern helps determine whether the issue is detector-related, workstation-related, generator-related, network-related, or workflow-related.

steps.title: Remove Failed Accessories from Service
steps.instructions: If one detector, battery, cable, charger, or network cable is clearly associated with the failure, remove that item from use. Label the suspect accessory. Document the serial number if available. Test the system with a known-good accessory. Expected outcome: The room is returned to service only if the remaining configuration passes functional testing. If the issue follows the accessory, send the accessory for repair or replacement.

CCR.Complaint: Technologist reported the Carestream DRX-Evolution would not complete the exam after exposure, and the acquired image did not display on the workstation.
CCR.Cause: Found the wireless DRX detector intermittently disconnecting due to a depleted detector battery.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced the detector battery with a charged known-good battery, confirmed detector connection, completed a test acquisition, and returned the room to service.

helpfulDetails: Alarm behavior, exact error message, or prompt displayed
Accessories swapped, including detector battery, detector, cable, charger, or network cable
Power behavior, including outlet or room power status and acquisition workstation restart
Environmental factors, including whether the issue affected one detector, one room position, one exam type, one technologist login, all exams, or all detectors
Indicator lights, including wired or wireless detector status, network link lights, generator ready status, or exposure inhibit status
Final device status, including whether the room was returned to service or removed from service

title: Carestream DRX-Evolution Radiographic System - Tube Stand, Wall Stand, or Table Tracking Problem
description: Tube stand, wall stand, or table tracking issues caused by setup, positioning, lock, sensor, accessory, or system communication problems.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot movement, tracking, or positioning problems while a patient is actively positioned for imaging. Action: Remove the patient from the table or move them to a safe position before testing system movement. Confirm the tube stand, wall stand, and table can be moved without contacting the patient, staff, accessories, or nearby equipment. Expected outcome: The area is safe before any mechanical movement or tracking test is performed. Why it matters: Tracking problems can cause unexpected positioning behavior, poor alignment, collision risk, or repeat imaging. If patient safety cannot be maintained, stop and remove the system from clinical use.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Tracking Problem
steps.instructions: Confirm exactly what staff mean by “tracking problem.” Action: Ask whether the issue involves: Tube stand not following the table. Tube stand not tracking the wall stand. Wall stand bucky not aligning. Table bucky not tracking properly. Detector/table position not recognized. Tube/table/wall stand alignment indicators not matching. Movement stops, jerks, or requires excessive manual correction. Attempt to reproduce the issue without a patient present. Expected outcome: The specific tracking failure is confirmed before troubleshooting continues. If the issue cannot be reproduced, document the reported behavior and continue with basic checks.

steps.title: Check for Physical Obstructions
steps.instructions: Inspect the tube stand, table, wall stand, and floor path for anything interfering with movement. Action: Check for: Cables caught under the table or tube stand. Foot pedals, stools, trash cans, linen carts, or accessories in the travel path. Debris in floor rails or around moving bases. Wall stand bucky obstruction. Table pads, straps, positioning aids, or detector holders preventing movement. Remove any obstruction and retest tracking. Expected outcome: Movement and tracking return to normal after the obstruction is removed. If this resolves the issue, return the system to service and document the obstruction found.

steps.title: Verify Mechanical Locks and Brakes Are Fully Released
steps.instructions: A partially engaged lock can make tracking appear failed. Action: Check that all relevant locks, brakes, and detents are released before attempting movement. Confirm: Tube stand longitudinal movement is unlocked. Tube angulation locks are released as needed. Wall stand bucky movement is unlocked. Table movement is not locked. Any emergency stop or movement inhibit condition is cleared. Retest the affected tracking function. Expected outcome: The component moves freely and tracks correctly. If movement is restored after releasing a lock, stop troubleshooting and document the finding.

steps.title: Check System Power and Startup Status
steps.instructions: Tracking may not work correctly if one part of the system is not fully powered, initialized, or communicating. Action: Confirm the radiographic system is fully powered on. Check for: Console startup complete. Generator ready status. Tube stand display or indicators active, if equipped. Table and wall stand powered and responsive. No startup errors or motion inhibit messages. Restart the system only if it is safe and allowed by site procedure. Expected outcome: All system components initialize normally with no motion or tracking errors. If a specific component does not power up or initialize, troubleshoot that power or communication issue first.

steps.title: Check Emergency Stop and Safety Interlock Status
steps.instructions: A safety stop or interlock may disable tracking or motion. Action: Inspect all accessible emergency stop buttons and safety interlocks. Confirm: Emergency stop buttons are not pressed. Collision sensors are not triggered. Table safety switches are not obstructed. Wall stand and tube stand travel limits are not reached. No service panel or access cover appears open or loose. Reset only normal operator-accessible emergency stop buttons according to site procedure. Expected outcome: Motion and tracking are available once the safety inhibit is cleared. If the system continues to report a safety inhibit with no visible cause, remove it from service.

steps.title: Verify Detector and Bucky Positioning
steps.instructions: Incorrect detector or bucky position can cause alignment or tracking complaints. Action: Check whether the selected image receptor matches the intended exam setup. Confirm: Correct detector is selected. Detector is seated properly in the table or wall stand bucky. Bucky tray is fully latched. Wireless detector battery is charged, if applicable. Detector is recognized by the acquisition workstation. Staff are not attempting table tracking while the system is set for wall stand imaging, or vice versa. Retest the tracking function after confirming setup. Expected outcome: The system recognizes the correct receptor and tracking behaves normally. If this resolves the issue, document the incorrect receptor or detector setup.

steps.title: Confirm Exam Room Setup and Selected Tracking Mode
steps.instructions: The system may behave correctly but not as expected if the wrong positioning mode is selected. Action: Check the acquisition workstation, tube stand controls, or room interface for the selected receptor or positioning mode. Verify the system is set for the intended configuration: Table bucky imaging. Wall stand imaging. Free detector imaging. Manual positioning. Auto-tracking or tube tracking mode, if available. Change only normal user-accessible settings. Expected outcome: Tube stand, table, or wall stand tracking matches the selected exam mode. If the tracking problem was caused by mode selection, return the system to service after verifying normal operation.

steps.title: Inspect External Cables and Connectors
steps.instructions: Loose or damaged cables can cause intermittent tracking, position feedback, or communication problems. Action: Visually inspect accessible external cabling between the table, wall stand, tube stand, generator, and workstation. Look for: Loose connectors. Damaged cable jackets. Pinched cables. Strain at connector ends. Cables pulled tight during movement. Cables near moving mechanical parts. Do not open panels or perform internal wiring repair during basic troubleshooting. Expected outcome: External cabling is secure and undamaged. If a cable is loose and accessible, reseat it if allowed by site procedure and retest. If damaged, remove the system from service.

steps.title: Check for Position Sensor or Alignment Indicator Problems
steps.instructions: Tracking depends on accurate position feedback. A sensor issue may show up as incorrect position, inconsistent alignment, or motion errors. Action: Move the affected component through a small safe range of travel and observe system feedback. Check whether: Position indicators update smoothly. Alignment lights or indicators respond correctly. The system detects table or wall stand position. Movement stops unexpectedly. Tracking works in one direction but not another. The error occurs only at certain positions. Expected outcome: Position feedback changes smoothly and matches physical movement. If feedback is missing, delayed, erratic, or position-dependent, the issue may involve a position sensor, encoder, cable, or control circuit. Escalate for service evaluation.

steps.title: Test Table Tracking Separately
steps.instructions: Isolate whether the issue follows the table movement. Action: With the room clear, move the table through normal operator-accessible motion. Observe whether: Table movement is smooth. Tube tracking follows as expected. Table locks release properly. Tracking fails only during tabletop movement. The table reports an error or stops unexpectedly. Expected outcome: Table movement is smooth and tube tracking follows appropriately. If the table movement is abnormal, the table may require service evaluation.

steps.title: Test Wall Stand Tracking Separately
steps.instructions: Isolate whether the issue follows the wall stand or wall bucky. Action: Move the wall stand bucky through its normal range, if safe. Observe whether: Wall stand bucky moves smoothly. Position feedback updates correctly. Tube tracking follows the wall stand position. Movement is restricted or noisy. The issue occurs at a repeatable height or position. Expected outcome: Wall stand movement and tracking are consistent. If the wall stand binds, slips, fails to report position, or causes repeated tracking errors, remove the system from service.

steps.title: Test Tube Stand Movement Separately
steps.instructions: Confirm the tube stand itself moves correctly before blaming table or wall stand tracking. Action: Move the tube stand manually through safe operator-accessible motion. Check: Longitudinal travel. Vertical travel. Transverse movement. Tube angulation. Detents or locks. Smoothness of motion. Any unusual noise, drag, or sudden stopping. Expected outcome: Tube stand movement is smooth, controlled, and position feedback remains stable. If tube stand movement is rough, restricted, or erratic, the problem may be mechanical or sensor-related and should be escalated.

steps.title: Check for Recent Changes
steps.instructions: Recent room changes often create tracking complaints. Action: Ask staff whether anything changed before the problem started. Check for: Detector replacement. Workstation update. Room calibration. Table service. Wall stand service. Tube stand collision. Construction, floor work, or equipment relocation. New positioning accessories. Expected outcome: Any recent change is identified and considered during escalation. If the problem began after service, software update, or collision, document that clearly in the work order.

steps.title: Perform a Safe Functional Check
steps.instructions: After external checks, perform a basic room function test without a patient. Action: Verify: Table tracking. Wall stand tracking. Manual tube positioning. Detector recognition. Alignment indication. No active motion, tracking, or interlock error. No unusual sound, smell, heat, vibration, or resistance. Expected outcome: Tracking operates normally and the room is safe for clinical use. If the issue is resolved and no abnormal condition remains, return the system to service.

CCR.Complaint: Radiology reported the Carestream DRX-Evolution tube stand was not tracking correctly with the wall stand during chest imaging setup.
CCR.Cause: Verified tracking failure with no patient present; no obstruction found, locks were released, detector was seated correctly, and wall stand position feedback appeared inconsistent.
CCR.Resolution: Removed room from service, labeled system Out of Service, documented failed wall stand tracking behavior, and escalated to vendor service for evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Whether the issue involved the tube stand, wall stand, table, or all tracking modes
Whether the problem occurred during table imaging, wall stand imaging, or both
Detector selected and whether it was recognized
Power behavior, outlet status, or system power status, if relevant
Any displayed error, alarm behavior, or motion inhibit message
Whether locks, brakes, or emergency stops were checked
Whether accessories were swapped, moved, or removed
Whether cables were inspected or reseated
Whether tracking failed in one direction or at one position
Any unusual noise, binding, vibration, heat, smell, environmental factors, or collision history
Any indicator lights, alignment indicators, or position feedback behavior
Whether the device was returned to service or removed from service

title: Carestream DRX-Evolution Radiographic System - Exposure Inhibited Due to Generator or System Interlock Fault
description: Troubleshooting guide for exposure inhibited conditions caused by room interlocks, generator readiness issues, tube stand position, detector status, or safety-related system faults on a Carestream DRX-Evolution radiographic system.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot exposure problems while the patient is positioned for imaging unless it is safe to continue. Stop the attempted exposure. Move the patient away from the table, wall stand, or tube area if troubleshooting requires repositioning equipment. Notify radiology staff that the system is not ready for exposure. Use another radiographic room or portable X-ray system if imaging is urgent. Expected outcome: The patient is protected from delayed imaging, repeated positioning, or attempted exposure on an inhibited system. Why it matters: An exposure inhibit is a safety stop. The system is preventing radiation output because one or more required conditions are not satisfied.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Exposure Inhibit
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact behavior before assuming a generator failure. Ask the technologist when the exposure inhibit occurs. Confirm whether the system prevents exposure at prep, rotor, or exposure. Check the operator console for the displayed warning, fault, or interlock message. Note whether the issue occurs on table exams, wall stand exams, or all exams. Expected outcome: The failure pattern is identified and documented.
