If the issue only occurs on one receptor or one exam type, focus troubleshooting on that specific setup.

steps.title: Check Room Door and Radiation Safety Interlocks
steps.instructions: Verify that all room safety interlocks are satisfied. Confirm the X-ray room door is fully closed. Check for any door interlock, exposure hold, or room-ready indicator issue. Inspect the door switch area for obvious damage, looseness, or misalignment. Ask staff if the issue began after door work, wall repair, or room maintenance. Expected outcome: The system recognizes the room as safe for exposure. If the door or room interlock clears the issue, test system readiness and return the room to service if operation is normal.

steps.title: Confirm Emergency Stop and Safety Switch Status
steps.instructions: Check for an engaged emergency stop or safety stop condition. Inspect visible emergency stop buttons in the room and control area. Confirm no E-stop is pressed or latched. Reset only if it is safe and appropriate to do so. Verify that the system returns to normal ready status after reset. Expected outcome: The system is no longer held in a safety-stop condition. If resetting an E-stop resolves the issue, document the finding and verify normal operation before returning the system to use.

steps.title: Verify System Power and Startup Status
steps.instructions: Confirm the radiographic system completed startup normally. Check the operator workstation, generator cabinet indicators, tube stand indicators, and wall stand or table status. Confirm there are no startup errors still active. Verify the generator appears powered and ready. Perform a controlled restart only if approved by site procedure and patient care is not affected. Expected outcome: All major system components are powered, communicating, and ready. If the system fails to complete startup or generator status remains abnormal, remove the room from service.

steps.title: Check Tube Stand, Wall Stand, and Table Positioning
steps.instructions: Exposure may be inhibited if the system does not recognize a valid imaging position. Confirm the tube is properly aligned to the selected receptor. Verify the tube stand, wall stand, and table are not in an unsafe or invalid position. Check whether detents, locks, or positioning indicators show ready status. Move the system out of and back into the expected exam position if safe. Expected outcome: The selected receptor and tube position are recognized as valid for exposure. If repositioning clears the inhibit, perform a test workflow and document the positioning-related cause.

steps.title: Confirm Detector or Receptor Selection
steps.instructions: Make sure the selected detector matches the intended exam setup. Verify the correct detector, wall stand, or table bucky is selected at the console. Confirm the detector is inserted, seated, charged, and available if using a DR detector. Check for detector communication or registration messages. Try a known-good detector or alternate receptor if available and appropriate. Expected outcome: The system recognizes a valid image receptor and allows exposure readiness. If the issue follows one detector, remove that detector from service and continue with a known-good detector if clinically acceptable.

steps.title: Check Collimator and Tube-Related Readiness
steps.instructions: Confirm that the tube and collimator are not causing a readiness inhibit. Verify the collimator is powered and responding. Check for visible collimator display, light field, or control issues. Confirm the tube is not reporting a thermal, rotation, filament, or housing-related warning. Ask staff whether the issue occurred after repeated high technique exposures. Expected outcome: Tube and collimator readiness conditions are normal. If a tube thermal or generator readiness message is present, allow appropriate cooldown if indicated by the system and retest only when safe.

steps.title: Verify Technique and Exam Selection
steps.instructions: Incorrect or unsupported technique settings can prevent exposure. Confirm the exam protocol is valid. Check kVp, mA, mAs, exposure mode, AEC selection, and detector selection. Make sure AEC is not selected for an exam setup that does not support it. Have the technologist select a simple known-good exam protocol and attempt prep readiness without exposing a patient. Expected outcome: The system accepts the selected technique and prepares normally. If changing to a valid protocol clears the inhibit, document the setup issue and return the system to service after functional verification.

steps.title: Check Hand Switch or Exposure Control
steps.instructions: A faulty or partially pressed exposure switch can cause prep or exposure problems. Inspect the hand switch or exposure control for obvious damage. Confirm the switch is not stuck, loose, or physically damaged. Ask staff whether prep occurs but exposure does not, or whether neither stage responds. Try an alternate approved exposure control if available. Expected outcome: Exposure control input is reliable and recognized by the system. If the issue follows the exposure switch, remove the switch from service and replace or escalate according to site process.

steps.title: Check for Active System Faults or Error History
steps.instructions: Review available system messages without entering deep service-level repair. Record the exact displayed fault, error code, or inhibit message. Note whether the fault references generator, tube, detector, interlock, door, table, wall stand, or system communication. Check whether the fault returns immediately after restart. Do not clear recurring safety faults without identifying the cause. Expected outcome: The fault is narrowed to an external condition, accessory issue, configuration problem, or likely internal system failure. If the fault repeatedly returns and no external cause is found, escalate for repair.

steps.title: Perform a Safe Functional Check
steps.instructions: Only test the system when the room is clear and site policy allows. Confirm no patient is present. Confirm the room is secured. Use the appropriate test workflow or phantom process per department policy. Verify prep, exposure readiness, exposure completion, and image acquisition. Expected outcome: The system exposes normally, captures the image, and no inhibit returns. If the exposure inhibit is resolved and the system passes functional checks, return the room to service.

CCR.Complaint: Radiology reported the Carestream DRX-Evolution would prep but would not expose due to an exposure inhibit message at the operator console.
CCR.Cause: Found the wall stand was not fully locked into the selected receptor position, preventing the system from reaching exposure-ready status.
CCR.Resolution: Repositioned and locked the wall stand, verified tube-to-receptor alignment, performed functional test exposure with no patient present, and returned the room to service.

helpfulDetails: Exact displayed inhibit message or error code
Whether prep occurs before the exposure is inhibited
Whether the issue affects table, wall stand, or all exams
Door interlock status
E-stop status
Tube stand and wall stand position
Selected receptor or detector
Detector battery and communication status
Technique selected
AEC selection status
Generator ready indicators
Alarm behavior or warning tones
Indicator lights on console, generator, table, or wall stand
Unusual sounds from generator, tube, or exposure control
Unusual heat or smell
Whether restart temporarily cleared the issue
Final device status

title: Carestream DRX-Evolution Radiographic System - PACS Send Failure or Worklist Connectivity Issue
description: Troubleshooting DRX-Evolution PACS send failures, missing worklist updates, network issues, destination errors, or failed image transfer.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot network transfer issues during active imaging if it delays patient care. Action: Confirm the exam is complete or pause troubleshooting until patient imaging is safely finished. If additional imaging is needed urgently, use another radiographic room or approved downtime workflow. Do not delete images, retry exams unnecessarily, or modify patient data without confirming clinical workflow requirements. Expected outcome: Patient care continues safely while image transfer or worklist troubleshooting is handled separately. If this resolves the workflow concern, stop.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Problem
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the issue is with PACS sending, worklist retrieval, or both. Action: Ask staff what failed: Images not sending to PACS. Images stuck in queue. Worklist not loading. Patient orders missing. Incorrect destination selected. Network or DICOM error displayed. Check whether the issue affects one patient, one exam, or all studies. Expected outcome: The failure type is clearly identified before troubleshooting continues.

steps.title: Check for Facility-Wide PACS or Network Problems
steps.instructions: A room issue should not be assumed until broader system problems are ruled out. Action: Ask if other imaging rooms are sending normally. Check whether PACS, RIS, or worklist services are known to be down. Contact imaging informatics or IT if multiple rooms are affected. Expected outcome: You determine whether the DRX-Evolution room is isolated or part of a larger outage. If multiple systems are affected, escalate to IT/imaging informatics and stop local equipment troubleshooting.

steps.title: Confirm the System Is on the Correct Network
steps.instructions: PACS and worklist communication depend on the acquisition workstation being connected to the correct hospital network. Action: Verify the network cable is fully seated at the workstation, wall jack, or network switch connection. Check for link/activity lights at the network port if visible. Confirm the system has not been moved, disconnected, or plugged into the wrong network jack. If wireless networking is used in the local setup, confirm it is connected to the correct clinical network. Expected outcome: The workstation has a valid physical or wireless network connection. If reconnecting the network restores worklist or PACS transfer, document the connection issue and stop.

steps.title: Restart the Acquisition Workstation Only If Safe
steps.instructions: A software or communication service may recover after a controlled restart. Action: Confirm no active acquisition is in progress. Save or protect any unsent images according to local workflow. Perform a normal shutdown and restart of the acquisition workstation. Recheck worklist and PACS send after startup. Expected outcome: Communication services reload and normal DICOM transfer or worklist query returns. If the restart resolves the issue, stop.

steps.title: Check the Worklist Search Criteria
steps.instructions: Missing worklist entries can be caused by search filters rather than system failure. Action: Confirm the correct date range is selected. Check modality, location, accession, patient name, and patient ID filters. Clear overly narrow filters and refresh the worklist. Confirm registration or order entry was completed in the upstream system. Expected outcome: The correct patient order appears if the issue was caused by filtering or incomplete order entry. If the worklist appears after correcting filters, stop.

steps.title: Verify DICOM Destination Selection
steps.instructions: Images may fail to send if the wrong PACS destination or archive route is selected. Action: Check the selected send destination on the DRX-Evolution workstation. Confirm staff did not select a test, offline, secondary, or incorrect destination. Retry send to the normal PACS destination if available. Avoid changing DICOM configuration unless authorized by imaging informatics or service support. Expected outcome: Images are routed to the correct configured PACS destination. If sending succeeds after selecting the correct destination, stop.

steps.title: Review the Send Queue or Failed Jobs
steps.instructions: The queue can show whether images are waiting, failed, rejected, or actively transferring. Action: Open the image transfer/send queue. Identify failed or pending studies. Note the error message, destination, time of failure, accession number, and patient/exam details. Retry one failed send if safe and appropriate. Do not repeatedly retry a large queue if the network or PACS is unavailable. Expected outcome: You identify whether the issue is a single failed study or a broader transfer failure.

steps.title: Confirm Patient and Exam Data Are Valid
steps.instructions: PACS may reject studies with missing or mismatched patient, accession, or exam information. Action: Check for missing accession number, incorrect patient ID, mismatched exam type, or duplicate study information. Compare the study information against the order in the RIS or EHR. Have imaging staff correct patient/order data using approved workflow if needed. Expected outcome: The study contains valid demographic and order information for PACS acceptance. If correcting demographics or order details allows the image to send, stop.

steps.title: Check for Local Storage or Queue Backup
steps.instructions: A full local drive or overloaded queue can prevent normal image transfer. Action: Check whether the workstation reports storage, archive, or queue capacity warnings. Ask staff whether multiple studies have been stuck for an extended period. Avoid deleting studies unless the images are confirmed archived and deletion is approved by department policy. Expected outcome: Storage or queue limitations are identified without risking image loss.

steps.title: Test Basic Network Function If Available
steps.instructions: Clinical Engineering should verify simple network indicators before assuming equipment failure. Action: Confirm the workstation shows network connectivity. Check whether other network functions on the system work, if available. If local policy allows, coordinate with IT to verify IP address, network jack, VLAN, and connectivity to PACS/worklist servers. Expected outcome: Basic network communication is either confirmed or escalated to IT.

steps.title: Escalate Configuration or Server Issues
steps.instructions: DICOM AE titles, IP addresses, ports, and server-side routing are usually controlled by imaging informatics, IT, or vendor service. Action: Escalate if: PACS destination fails repeatedly. Worklist cannot query. DICOM association errors appear. AE title, IP, or port errors are suspected. Other rooms are working but this room cannot communicate. Provide exact error messages, screenshots if permitted, and affected accession examples. Expected outcome: The correct support team can troubleshoot DICOM routing, PACS, RIS, firewall, or workstation configuration.

CCR.Complaint: Radiology reported that the Carestream DRX-Evolution could not send completed studies to PACS and the modality worklist was not updating.
CCR.Cause: Found network cable loose at the acquisition workstation, causing intermittent PACS and worklist communication failure.
CCR.Resolution: Reseated network cable, verified link lights, restarted the workstation, refreshed worklist, and confirmed test study successfully transferred to PACS.

helpfulDetails: Whether other radiographic rooms were affected
Alarm behavior, system messages, or DICOM error behavior
Accessories swapped or network components checked
Power behavior or workstation restart behavior
Environmental factors such as recent room moves, cable disturbance, network work, or downtime events
Indicator lights, including network link/activity lights
PACS send queue status
Worklist behavior
Exact error messages displayed
Network cable or wall jack checked
Destination selected
Patient/order filters checked
Accession or demographic mismatch found
Study successfully resent or still pending
IT/imaging informatics ticket number if escalated
Final device status
